data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Monday at 11 AM ET
data.description:
Dow, Nasdaq Fall After Trump Doesn't Rule Out Recession

Major U.S. stock markets tumbled after President Trump refused to rule out the U.S. economy entering a recession this year.

Trump Declines to Rule Out Recession

In an interview that aired Sunday, the president demurred when asked if there were reassurances he could provide to businesses seeking more clarity on tariffs.

The former central banker will officially become Canada's new leader in the coming days and take over his country's response to President Trump's trade war.

Southern yellow pine futures will begin trading later this month as prices split from those of Canadian lumber.

German Industrial Production Rose at Start of 2025 as Tariff Threats Loom

Output increased 2% on month in January as German industry showed tentative signs of improvement, though that could be threatened by U.S. trade tariffs.

China Struggles to Shake Off Disinflationary Pressure as Trade Threats Loom

What the Dot-Com Bust Can Tell Us About Today's AI Boom

This week marks the 25th anniversary of the Nasdaq peak, but all wasn't lost in the ensuing downturn.

Markets Finally Woke Up to Tariff Reality. Is This a Buying Opportunity?

Your correspondent remains torn, but if you believe in the Trump pitch, now might be a time to take a swing.

How European Defense Stocks Became a Hedge-Fund Home Run

Drastic shifts in the global security environment have sparked a sector rally.

Tariff Wars Are Often Short. Their Legacies Aren't.

Economists fear that Trump's tariffs, once they take hold, could have unexpected effects long after he leaves office.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Monday at 11 AM ET
data.description:
Oil prices consolidate after 7th straight weekly loss for U.S. benchmark

Oil futures were holding small gains early Monday, consolidating after a seventh straight weekly loss for the U.S. crude benchmark tied to uncertainty over trade policy and other worries.

Whitecap Resources, Veren to Merge in a $10.4 Billion All-Share Deal

Whitecap Resources and Veren have agreed to combine their companies in $10.44 billion deal that will enlarge their Alberta oil and gas footprint.

Eneos Plans $3 Billion IPO of Metals Subsidiary

The Tokyo-based energy giant said that it plans to offer a majority of shares in the wholly owned subsidiary, JX Advanced Metals, in the offering that would raise as much as 438.6 billion yen, equivalent to $2.96 billion.

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on the uranium outlook and more in the latest Market Talks covering energy and utilities.

Nearly Half of Energy Executives Think We Won't Reach Net Zero Until 2070

Rising capital project costs and a lack of optimism around ESG are pushing back environmental goals, according to a survey of industry leaders from across oil and gas, utilities, chemicals, mining, and agribusiness.

Harbour Energy Shares Sink to Record Low After Profit Hit by U.K. Windfall Tax

The oil-and-gas producer swung to a loss in 2024 as the energy profits levy continued to drag on results and its CEO described the U.K. as a difficult fiscal and regulatory environment in which to operate.

Sunnova Bondholders Engage Advisers Following KKR Deal, Going-Concern Warning

A group of bondholders in Sunnova Energy International has organized and hired legal counsel after the rooftop solar panel company issued a going concern notice and said it raised financing from KKR.

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Rise More Than Expected

U.S. crude oil inventories rose by 3.6 million barrels last week, which was more than expected, resuming an upward trend after a surprise drawdown the week before.

DTEK in Talks With U.S. Suppliers to Boost Gas Supply to Europe

Ukraine's largest private energy company is in discussions with U.S. suppliers to bring more natural gas to Europe as part of a broader expansion, its CEO said in an interview.

Nuclear Power's Revival Is Here. What Do You Do With All the Radioactive Waste?

Tons of radioactive refuse go into temporary storage every year, and new efforts to store nuclear waste away from reactor sites are getting pushback.

data.title: February US Median Inflation Expectations Accelerate for Short Term, Unchanged for Medium Term, According to NY Fed Survey
data.description:
Consumer expectations for one-year US inflation growth accelerated to a 3.1% gain in February from a 3% gain in the previous three months, according to a survey released by the New York Federal Reserve Bank on Monday.

The median inflation expectations were unchanged from a 3% gain for both the three-year period and the five years ahead.

The median inflation uncertainty jumped to 4% from 3.4% in the one-year horizon and rose to 3.7% from 3.2% for the three-year outlook.

Median home price change expectations increased to 3.3% from 3.2% while home price uncertainty increased to a 3.9% increase from a 3.6% increase in the previous month.

The median one-year expectations commodity prices growth increased for all the components which include food, gas, medical care, cost of college education, rent and gold.

data.title: Genmab Shares Fall After J&J Drops Blood-Cancer Treatment Option
data.description:
By Michael Susin

Genmab shares fell on Monday after the company said it would end the development of blood-cancer treatment HexaBody-CD38 because Johnson & Johnson won't exercise its option to license it.

The stock was down 5.4% at 15.70 Danish Krone in European afternoon trading, having fallen to a low of 14.87 krone shortly after the announcement was made. Shares are currently up 4.8% over the year to date.

The Danish biotechnology company said Johnson & Johnson won't exercise its option to receive a worldwide license to develop, manufacture and commercialize HexaBody-CD38.

Genmab said the decision was made after evaluating the data, the market landscape and its portfolio prioritization policies and despite the clinical potential of the treatment. Genmab said preliminary data showed positive partial response to HexaBody-CD38.

HexaBody-CD38 was currently being researched in a midstage clinical study in patients with multiple myeloma and other blood cancers.

The decision doesn't impact Genmab's 2025 financial targets, it added.

Write to Michael Susin at michael.susin@wsj.com

data.title: Canada to Launch U.S.-Dollar Bond Tuesday
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canada says it intends to issue a U.S.-dollar-denominated bond Tuesday, aimed at maintaining liquid foreign reserves at or above 3% of nominal output.

Canada's Department of Finance said the bond would provide funds to diversify foreign reserves, and help promote "orderly market conditions" for the Canadian dollar in foreign-exchange markets.

No terms were released, such as the size of the offering and yield.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: Eli Lilly to Roll Out Weight-loss Drug Mounjaro in China, Other Emerging Markets by 2026
data.description:
Eli Lilly's Chief Financial Officer Lucas Montarce said the group is preparing to roll out its drug Mounjaro, or tirzepatide, in several emerging markets including China, India, Brazil and Mexico by 2026, Reuters reported Monday.

Mounjaro is sold in the UK and Europe under the same brand name for both weight loss and diabetes.

data.title: RWE Cuts 73 Jobs at U.S. Offshore Wind Division
data.description:
By Adam Whittaker

German utility company RWE is cutting 73 jobs in its U.S. offshore wind division in Massachusetts.

Last year, RWE delayed investments in its U.S. offshore wind projects due to "market conditions and increased risk profile."

U.S. President Trump has halted new leases for offshore wind projects and has been critical of the sector.

RWE said in a statement Monday that it cut the roles due to the current regulatory and political environment.

RWE employs around 1,500 people in the U.S.

Write to Adam Whittaker at adam.whittaker@wsj.com

data.title: Eurozone's Relative Predictability Makes Euro Assets More Attractive Than U.S. Peers — Market Talk
data.description:
1110 ET - Eurozone assets appear more attractive than their U.S. counterparts due to the relative predictability in the eurozone compared to the U.S., ABN Amro senior U.S. economist Rogier Quaedvlieg says. Since the inauguration of President Trump, uncertainty has soared. "With EU policy uncertainty effectively doubling, while U.S. policy uncertainty [has] quadrupled", he writes in a note. The U.S. is likely to suffer the biggest impact from increased unpredictability, Quaedvlieg says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1039 ET - Cracker Barrel has space to keep raising prices without turning customers away from the brand, Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks says in a research note. The chain is still offering a better value relative to most of its casual-dining and family-dining competitors, he says. Management shared data showing an average check at Cracker Barrel ran $15 last quarter, compared with $28 for casual-dining peers and $18 for family-dining rivals, the analyst says. He maintains a hold rating on the stock, given that Cracker Barrel is still in the early steps of its three-year turnaround. Shares rise 7%. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1030 ET - Cracker Barrel Old Country Store is already showing progress in the early stages of its turnaround, Truist analysts Jake Bartlett and Larson Rice say in a research note. Solid results in the last two quarters haven't been reflected in the stock's valuation, they say. Cracker Barrel has innovated its menu and improved service levels in a way that has allowed them to raise prices while improving traffic, customer satisfaction scores and margins, the analysts say. The gains have all come ahead of a remodel program that should provide another boost, they say. The analysts upgrade the stock to a buy from hold. Shares rise 7.2%.(dean.seal@wsj.com)

1018 ET - The Fed has signaled it wants to be careful about running down its balance sheet while the debt-ceiling debate unfolds in Congress, because the drawdown of the Treasury's bank account could cover up a background shortage of bank reserves that would only become apparent once the debt ceiling is lifted or suspended. The Fed could instead choose to slow runoff to a crawl without stopping it entirely, LHMeyer analysts write. That plan would still curtail the risks, while doing more to signal the Fed's strong intention to continue running off the balance sheet. "To pause with a presumption to resume, only never to do so, might undermine the Fed's credibility on balance sheet guidance," LHMeyer writes. (matt.grossman@wsj.com)

1011 ET - European stocks are more attractive than their U.S. peers due to the potential boost from higher defense spending by eurozone governments, XTB research director Kathleen Brooks says in a note. European defense stocks are likely to continue rallying as governments raise spending, she says. "The European defense sector could have further to go, even if it runs into roadblocks on its upward journey." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0956 ET - Tariffs likely helped keep inflation above the Fed's 2% target last month, Bank of America's Stephen Juneau and Jeseo Park write. They forecast year-over-year CPI, due Wednesday, at 2.9%, compared to January's 3%, with the core slowing to 3.2% from 3.3%. One driver is the additional 10% tariff on imports from China, they say. BofA expects prices of core goods ex-used cars to accelerate, since much of these items are imported from China. If tariffs don't impact this month, the impact will likely be felt in following months Juneau and Park write. If their forecast is correct, there will be "little reason" for the Fed to cut rates. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0954 ET - Cryptocurrencies had a down week, with total market cap off 1.8% in the past 24 hours and down roughly 13% in the past 30 days, according to data from CoinMarketCap. In the past week, the market seems to have been selling off following President Trump's announcement of a strategic bitcoin reserve--including a crypto summit held at the White House on Friday. Markets appear underwhelmed with the news out of Washington, but the fall of bitcoin to a recent low of roughly $82,000 may soon turn around, says Nigel Green of the deVere Group. "The market's reaction betrays a short-sightedness that will likely be corrected as investors digest the broader implications of the move," says Green in a note. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0939 ET - U.K. government plans to raise defense spending are boosting the performance of the country's defense stocks, driving them to perform better than U.S. tech stocks, XTB research director Kathleen Brooks says in a note. "Rolls Royce is the best performing stock on the FTSE 100 for 2025 so far, as U.K. defense firms continue to outpace big tech in the U.S." she says. The U.K. multinational aerospace and defense company's stock price is up by 30% since the start of 2025. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0901 ET - Tourism data indicate that anger in Canada over the Trump administration's tariff threats might be curtailing visits to the U.S. Statistics Canada says the number of trips by automobile by Canadians into the US and back to Canada fell sharply in February, down 23%, from a year ago. The Canadian data agency says this represented the second consecutive month of year-over-year declines and the second decline observed since March 2021. Canadians returning from the US by air also fell in February, down 13.1% from a year ago. Trump's tariff threats have prompted Canadians to boycott US goods, and alter vacation plans to the US. Canada is the top source of foreign visitors to the US, says the US Travel Association. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0856 ET - The dollar weakens as markets grapple with recession fears. The ICE's DXY dollar index is on path for its lowest close since early November. The initial euphoria around President Trump's promised market-friendly tax cuts and deregulation gives way to concerns about tariffs possibly boosting inflation, keeping interest rates high and crippling economic growth. Declining Treasury yields, meanwhile, remove a key support for the greenback. The dollar weakens 0.7% versus the yen and around 0.1% versus the euro and the pound. It strengthens 0.3% against the Canadian dollar. U.S. inflation data is due later this week. February CPI is expected to slow a little, in a WSJ survey. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0832 ET - Investors rush back to the safety of U.S. government debt, driving Treasury yields lower, amid fears of economic weakness. President Trump stops short of ruling out a recession, sounding less optimistic than some of his advisers. Former central banker Mark Carney wins the leadership of Canada's Liberal Party on promises to buck Trump's agenda. China's inflation is lower than expected. U.S. consumer and wholesale prices data are due this week. The 10-year yield is at 4.230%, giving up most of the gains recorded after Fed Chair Powell confirmed the central-bank wait-and-see stance on Friday. The two-year is at 3.933%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0726 ET - Futures linked to the tech-heavy Nasdaq-100 are down on Monday after President Trump refused to rule out the U.S. economy could be entering a recession this year in a weekend Fox News interview. The E-mini Nasdaq 100 futures contract is down 1.3%. Nvidia and Apple shares are down 2.4% and 1.4% premarket, respectively. In Europe, semiconductor stocks are in decline, with Dutch supplier of semiconductor-making equipment ASML Holding down 1.8%, smaller rival ASM International losing 3.2% and German chip maker Infineon Technologies shedding 3.1%. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: Update: MicroStrategy Shares Fall After Launching $21 Billion At-The-Market Stock Offering
data.description:
(Updates with the latest stock price movement in the headline and first paragraph.)

MicroStrategy's shares were down more than 12% in recent Monday trading after the company said it launched an up to $21 billion at-the-market offering for its 8% Series A perpetual strike preferred stock, which is convertible into class A common shares.

Net proceeds will be used for general corporate purposes, including bitcoin purchases and working capital, the company said.
